item.title: Fuel importers receive ‘portion’ of subsidy
item.description: FUEL importers have received “a significant part of their subsidies” from the Government allowing service stations to return to operational levels last weekend, an official says. Richard Kassman, the senior vice-president of the PNG Chamber of Resources & Energy (PNG Core), said:  “We confirm that fuel importers Puma Energy PNG Limited, Mobil Oil New Guinea Limited, Islands Petroleum Limi
item.content:encoded:
FUEL importers have received “a significant part of their subsidies” from the Government allowing service stations to return to operational levels last weekend, an official says.

Richard Kassman, the senior vice-president of the PNG Chamber of Resources & Energy (PNG Core), said:  “We confirm that fuel importers Puma Energy PNG Limited, Mobil Oil New Guinea Limited, Islands Petroleum Limited and Niugini Oil Company have received a significant part of their subsidies ensuring supply to next week.

“We are confident that the Government will fulfil its commitment to fund subsidies in advance to June 7, this year.”

On behalf of the fuel importers and suppliers, Kassman welcomed Prime Minister James Marape’s intervention in addressing fuel supply disruptions around the country over the weekend.

Marape and Treasurer Ian Ling-Stuckey met with industry executives led by Mobil Oil and Puma to discuss process improvements to ensure a seamless flow of funds to ensure normal operations along the fuel distribution chain.

PNG Core clarified that PNG was not facing a fuel supply shortage.

But the fuel industry will work with Rural and Economic Development Minister Joseph Lelang, the chairman of the State Task Force overseeing the fuel crisis, to ensure that the fuel subsidy programme is workable and sustainable.

Kassman said that the fuel subsidy programme required substantial upfront financial commitments, including foreign exchange arrangements secured months in advance.

He added that this represented “a significant undertaking by the government”, while noting that governments in other nearby countries have introduced only partial subsidies, leaving consumers to bear a larger share of fuel costs.

“By comparison, the people of PNG continue to benefit from relatively low fuel prices reflecting the government’s commitment to easing the burden on households and businesses,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Tsunami alert issued as ashes seen rising
item.description: THE submarine eruption at the Titan Ridge Volcano in Papua New Guinea’s north-central Bismarck Sea has escalated, with ash now confirmed in the plume, a development scientists warn could increase the risk of a tsunami. According to the reports obtained from the Rabaul Volcanological Observatory (RVO), on Saturday morning before heavy atmospheric cloud obscured the site, observers recorded the firs
item.content:encoded:
THE submarine eruption at the Titan Ridge Volcano in Papua New Guinea’s north-central Bismarck Sea has escalated, with ash now confirmed in the plume, a development scientists warn could increase the risk of a tsunami.

According to the reports obtained from the Rabaul Volcanological Observatory (RVO), on Saturday morning before heavy atmospheric cloud obscured the site, observers recorded the first signs of ash rising to 3,600-metres at 8.30am, coinciding with a magnitude 4.3 earthquake.

A larger ash pulse followed at 10.50am, accompanied by a stronger 5.1 magnitude earthquake.

The RVO report stated that since then, poor weather had made direct observation difficult, though thermal anomalies and pumice rafts confirm the eruption was continuing.

Satellite data shows a five-kilometre alignment of hotspots trending west-southwest to east-northeast.

Some of these may be floating pumice, which continues to spread west and northwest of the eruption site.

Maritime authorities have warned shipping operators to avoid the area, as pumice rafts pose hazards to vessels.

A magnitude 5.4 earthquake struck the site at 11.13am on Monday followed by two more tremors in the past 24 hours.

These events are believed to be linked to the onset of ash emissions.

Although the eruption is occurring in a remote location unlikely to directly impact communities, ashfall is expected in downwind areas.

Scientists caution that the eruption’s evolution from steam and pumice to ash, suggests the volcano may be breaking the sea surface, raising the possibility of tsunamis.

Authorities continue to urge vigilance, warning boat operators and shipping to steer clear as the Bismarck Sea rumbles offshore.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Six new electorates prepare for polls in GE27
item.description: VOTERS in the six new electorates proposed by the Electoral Boundaries Commission (EBC) are expected to go to the polls in General Election 2027 (GE27). The EBC, empowered by the Constitution and the Organic Law on National and Local Level Government Elections, conducted an electoral boundary and population redistribution review of the country after a national census.
item.content:encoded:
VOTERS in the six new electorates proposed by the Electoral Boundaries Commission (EBC) are expected to go to the polls in General Election 2027 (GE27).

The EBC, empowered by the Constitution and the Organic Law on National and Local Level Government Elections, conducted an electoral boundary and population redistribution review of the country after a national census.

In its report approved by Parliament in 2022, the EBC recommended the creation of 13 new open electorates – seven of which had come into effect during the general election that year.

They were Nakanai (West New Britain), Delta-Fly (Western), Hiri-Koiari (Central), Margarima (Hela), Porgera-Paiela (Enga), Popondetta (Northern) and Wau-Waria (Morobe).

The six electorates of Motu-Koita (National Capital District), Baniara (Milne Bay), Baining (East New Britain), Middle Sepik (East Sepik), Mendi Central (Southern Highlands) and Anglimp (Jiwaka) will be prepared for GE27.

Acting Electoral Commissioner Margaret Vagi recently stated that the commission was gearing up for GE27 with a nationwide voter enrolment programme that had started this month. Vagi, also the EBC chairwoman, said the six new electorates planned to go to the polls in GE27 would also participate in this programme so that all eligible voters could exercise their democratic right to vote.

“We have started with Motu-Koita in March, followed by Baniara,” she said.

“The remaining four electorates will be completed by June, ensuring readiness for the upcoming elections.”

She said that the commission worked with provincial administrations and other relevant stakeholders to ensure the correct placements of local level governments (LLGs) and wards within the new electorates.

“It is important to ensure the LLGs and wards are placed in the new electorates as this will see movement of voters to the right wards to confirm their names are on the electoral roll so they are eligible to vote.

“This process is critical to guarantee that voters are registered in the right wards and can confirm their names on the electoral roll.

“The Electoral Commission is committed to ensuring that the new electorates are ready for GE27,” Vagi said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Students to pay K40,000 for damages
item.description: STUDENTS of a secondary school in Western Highlands are to raise K40,000 this week to pay for damages caused to a police vehicle and school computers on Friday. In a meeting yesterday in Mt Hagen, Kui Wamp Secondary School board and parents of students agreed to raise the money today by contributing K10 for each student to pay the amount by tomorrow.
item.content:encoded:
STUDENTS of a secondary school in Western Highlands are to raise K40,000 this week to pay for damages caused to a police vehicle and school computers on Friday.

In a meeting yesterday in Mt Hagen, Kui Wamp Secondary School board and parents of students agreed to raise the money today by contributing K10 for each student to pay the amount by tomorrow.

Friday’s incident saw some students stone a police vehicle, breaking its window.

Provincial police commander Chief Superintendent John Sagom on Monday said the vehicle had been driven to the school by rural zones commander Inspector Moses Kalandi to attend to a complaint in the school when the incident happened.

Insp Kalandi, a 40-year veteran of the police force, was struck in the shoulder.

In the meeting, parents agreed for each of the school’s 4,000 students to contribute K10 immediately to raise K40,000 to pay for the damage to the vehicle.

Deputy board chairman Paul Rumints said Friday’s incident was related to an earlier incident involving a former teacher, a local from Kagamuga, who had entered the school’s administration office and damaged to two computers.

He said when the police were called in, some students sided with the former teacher who was arrested, and stoned the police vehicle, injuring Insp Kalandi.

“Police are here to assist and protect properties and lives. Yet some students decided to stone the police vehicle, which was wrong,” he said.

Rumints said it was important to resolve the situation as the school’s board and parents did not want the issue to disrupt or affect the grade 10 national written expression examination which is scheduled for next month.

He added that the school could not continue to send students home every time an issue arose and called for a change of attitudes from the staff, the student body as well as the community.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Barras advance. PNG top standings in East Asia Pacific qualifier
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea Barramundis emerged from the May 12-18 East Asia Pacific (EAP) qualifier in Japan as League Champions, securing them a place in the Asia-EAP regional final, where they will join Nepal, Oman, and the UAE in the battle for three coveted slots at the 2028 ICC Men’s T20 World Cup. Over the course of the tournament, the Barramundis produced commanding performances, including a towering
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea Barramundis emerged from the May 12-18 East Asia Pacific (EAP) qualifier in Japan as League Champions, securing them a place in the Asia-EAP regional final, where they will join Nepal, Oman, and the UAE in the battle for three coveted slots at the 2028 ICC Men’s T20 World Cup.

Over the course of the tournament, the Barramundis produced commanding performances, including a towering 207 against Vanuatu, and despite falling to Japan in the final Super Six clash, their superior record and net run rate ensured they topped the table.

Japan’s story runs parallel but with a different flavour. Hosting the qualifier, they built momentum through wins against Fiji, Vanuatu, and Samoa, and then capped their campaign with a 26 run victory over PNG in Sano. That result was historic: it marked Japan’s first progression into the regional final, a breakthrough that signals their arrival on the international stage.

Together, PNG and Japan now stand at the threshold of the next phase.

PNG, seasoned and confident, will look to extend their tradition of World Cup appearances.

Japan, fresh and ambitious, will aim to turn their home triumph into a lasting legacy.

The regional final next year promises to be a crucible where established contenders and new challengers collide, with only three advancing to Australia and New Zealand in 2028.

PNG secured three wins from four matches, earning eight points and edging hosts Japan on net run rate. Their dominant campaign included victories against Indonesia, Cook Islands, Samoa, and Vanuatu, with only one defeat in their final match against Japan.

The Barramundis finished with a net run rate of +2.1200, scoring 751 runs across 100 overs while restricting opponents to 539 runs. Japan also ended on eight points but trailed on net run rate, handing PNG the League Champion title.

1. PNG Barramundis – 8 points (+2.1200 NRR)

4. Cook Islands – 6 points (-0.5812 NRR)

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Panthers schools’ games launched
item.description: THE Rabaul Panthers Schools Competition in Kokopo, East New Britain, launched its second term last Friday, a programme aimed at fostering unity through sports while providing a pathway for youths to pursue professional rugby league. The competition began last year with four schools participating: Tavtavul, Waterhouse and Clifton Primary Schools, along with Waterhouse Junior High School. Kokopo Pri
item.content:encoded:
THE Rabaul Panthers Schools Competition in Kokopo, East New Britain, launched its second term last Friday, a programme aimed at fostering unity through sports while providing a pathway for youths to pursue professional rugby league.

The competition began last year with four schools participating: Tavtavul, Waterhouse and Clifton Primary Schools, along with Waterhouse Junior High School. Kokopo Primary School joined the competition this year.

From the 2025 competition, 12 students were selected for the National Rugby League (NRL) bid programme. Two boys from the Under-19 division were also chosen to join the Agmark Gurias Under-19 team. The programme, previously known as the NRL bid programme, has now transitioned into the PNG Rugby Football League (RFL) Academy.

The competition will feature eight rounds before the finals, with round one beginning this week.

Speaking during the launch, Agmark Gurias general manager Paul Joseph encouraged students to take the sport seriously and recognise the opportunities it can create.

“Sport is not only about going out there and playing. There are many attributes that contribute to success in sport,” he said. “Some of you seated here and others who will join later will go on that same pathway.”

Joseph added that the success of national rugby league players should inspire students to work hard and remain disciplined.

“To be a successful woman, rugby player or leader in any sport, your preparation starts at home,” he said.

Meanwhile, PNGRFL participation officer for the New Guinea Islands (NGI) region Solomon Darius thanked the participating schools and urged cooperation in coordinating games in the coming weeks.

He also acknowledged associations in other districts for promoting sports in their communities.

“Small programmes like this are important,” he said.

Divisions receiving special attention include the Under-15, Under-17 and Under-19 boy’s divisions, along with the Under-19 girl’s division. Selections for the PNG Academy will come from these divisions.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pacific Corporate renews gold sponsorship
item.description: THE United Sinesine-Yongomugl Rugby Football League (USSYRFL) has secured a major boost for its 2026 season, with Pacific Corporate Group of Companies renewing its gold sponsorship worth K50,000. The announcement was made in Kundiawa by medical doctor John Tonar, representing the company.
item.content:encoded:
THE United Sinesine-Yongomugl Rugby Football League (USSYRFL) has secured a major boost for its 2026 season, with Pacific Corporate Group of Companies renewing its gold sponsorship worth K50,000.

The announcement was made in Kundiawa by medical doctor John Tonar, representing the company.

This marks the second time Pacific Corporate Group has taken the gold sponsor role since the league’s establishment in 2024. In its inaugural year, the Sinesine-Yongomugl District Development Authority was the gold sponsor, while Pacific Corporate served as silver sponsor.

Tonar praised the depth of rugby talent in the district, noting the company’s commitment to nurturing players who could progress to the Digicel Cup, the Simbu Warriors, and ultimately the PNG Kumuls or overseas competitions such as the NRL.

He highlighted legends like Bal Numapo, Michael Mark, and Justin Olam as inspirations, stressing the importance of sports, health, and education for young people.

Pacific Corporate has a longstanding tradition of community investment, supporting charities, churches, education, and sports.

“We are people-oriented and always happy to invest in the development of people and the good of society,” Tonar said, pledging continued support for the league.

USSYRFL chairman Paul John Ore welcomed the sponsorship, calling it a milestone achievement for the two-year-old competition.

He said the investment was already bearing fruit, with two USSY graduates advancing to the Digicel Cup.

“Investment in rugby league is also an investment in development,” Ore said, reaffirming the league’s vision to nurture young talent.

Technical director Jamie Kola Mul and president Pastor John Kamane echoed the gratitude, noting that Pacific Corporate’s steady backing has been crucial since the league’s launch. The funds will support player development, competition costs, and outreach programmes, strengthening pathways from grassroots rugby to provincial and national representation.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: District backs Imbonggu league
item.description: THE Imbonggu District Development Authority has strengthened its support for youth sports by presenting a cheque of K30,000 to the Imbonggu Proper Rugby League last Saturday. Alongside this, the authority committed K50,000, cementing its role as the league’s major sponsor. During the presentation at Kapatumbu Rugby League Oval, local MP and Attorney-General Pila Niningi highlighted the importance
item.content:encoded:
THE Imbonggu District Development Authority has strengthened its support for youth sports by presenting a cheque of K30,000 to the Imbonggu Proper Rugby League last Saturday. Alongside this, the authority committed K50,000, cementing its role as the league’s major sponsor.

During the presentation at Kapatumbu Rugby League Oval, local MP and Attorney-General Pila Niningi highlighted the importance of sports in youth development.

He said young people from Imbonggu and Upper Nebilyer in Western Highlands will participate in the competition. The Western Highlands government also contributed K10,000 to the league.

Established last year, the Imbonggu Proper Rugby League is officially registered under the Papua New Guinea Rugby Football League, operating as a structured competition.

Niningi urged youths to avoid illicit drugs and focus on discipline through sports.

“Rugby league is a physical game that requires strong and disciplined youths. Some of you could be scouted to higher levels if you remain committed,” he said.

District executive officer Paul Kereme thanked PNGRFL regional president James Kundi for his ongoing support.

He reminded players that rugby can be a pathway to a career.

He encouraged them to follow the example of PNG stars Justin Olam and Edwin Ipape, who rose from local competitions to international representation.

Meanwhile, the Pangia Tu-Toni competition kicked off at Pangia Oval with eight clubs this season.

League president Bani Piku confirmed that teams including Topa Eels, Kia Broncos, Yambi Tarangau, K Walea Panthers, Kia Warriors, S Tigers, Wiru Mintis, and MK Rabbithos are fielding junior and senior squads in both male and female divisions.  Sponsor Wai Omola also provided match officials’ uniforms and office stationery to support the competition.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: A prison for Gulf will be victory for justice
item.description: THE verdict was handed down at the fall of the gavel. The assurance is, therefore, given and is legal: Gulf will finally have a provincial prison of its own after years of sending its lawbreakers to Port Moresby’s Bomana prison.
item.content:encoded:
THE verdict was handed down at the fall of the gavel.

The assurance is, therefore, given and is legal: Gulf will finally have a provincial prison of its own after years of sending its lawbreakers to Port Moresby’s Bomana prison.

The prison has to be built and made ready for use before the 2026 calendar year ends.

So ruled the National Court in Port Moresby last week.

Any wavering from that determination, without reasonable explanation, will be in breach of a court order, and be tantamount to contempt of court.

Unless, otherwise prevented by clever legal representation by those asked to come up with the cost for the prison, there seems no escaping the responsibility; all parties or authorities responsible should and will have to set aside time and resources and ensure the prison is established in the given time.

Justice Nicholas Miviri, a son of Gulf himself — specifically from Moveave village in Malalaua —  made the order while ruling on a human rights application brought to court by 76 detainees from the province who are being held at the Bomana prison because Gulf does not have a prison of its own.

In that ruling the judge ordered the national government and the offices of Gulf Governor Sir Christopher Haiveta, Kerema Open MP Thomas Opa (who is Minister for Finance), Kikori MP Soroi Eoe (Minister for Provincial and Local Government Affairs) to contribute a total of K80 million to build a prison facility in the province by December.

Each of those offices will be required to pay the K80 million in equal parts to build the jail.

The facility should be able to accommodate 400 prisoners and 130 prison officers.

The court also ordered the upfront payment of K1.8 million for the acquisition of land of Mupa in Malalaua.

Parties named in the human rights application and the judge’s orders will be required to report progress on the work done on the prison in the form of court affidavits to prove the execution of those orders. The orders will be reviewed on Aug 10, Oct 12 and Dec 16 at the Kerema National Court, according to our news report yesterday.

The court has made the order but the usual big question that will still hang over that is that of money. How soon work will start and perhaps more importantly, whether any work begun will be completed, and on time to be in compliance with the order, will hang on the availability of money.

This National Court ruling sets a precedent of sorts for those provinces or government entities at large who, for whatever reason, keep procrastinating what should be critical capital investments to better serve the public.

The situation in Gulf is not an isolated case.

Enga has for years been sending its prisoners to Baisu jail in Western Highlands.

Work on its Enga’s own prison at Mukurumanda, along the Highway between Wapenamanda and Wabag has been going on for 28 years owing to differences between the Correctional Institution Services and the provincial government.

Meanwhile, Enga prisoners were still being sent to Baisu which was obviously overcrowded and the bigger issue there was that Baisu’s facilities were completely run down.

The situation also caught the attention of the National Court and Justice Graham Ellis gave orders on March 5 this year for work on the Mukurumanda facility to be completed by end of June.

The matter will return to court on June 30 for the court to determine the progress of the work and whether the new prison will be able to take in prisoners.

The intervention of the courts in what should otherwise be important projects in the country’s law and justice sector is indicative of something between deliberate negligence or lack of commitment and or prioritisation by government entities inclusive of provincial governments.

The courts should be commended for taking decisive action where government entities appear reluctant to prioritise and fund important institutions for the maintenance of law and order such as prison camps.

Investing in prisons may be in the minds of some people, an unnecessary afterthought but there are compelling reasons why such investments are crucial for the overall wellbeing of the country.

Two pretty obvious reasons come to mind.

First, prisoner welfare borders on human rights.

Prisoners are essentially persons who have unfortunately found themselves on the wrong side of the law.

Second, overcrowded and run down prisons only increase the risk of prison breakouts, reoffending and a general threat to order, peace and prosperity.

That the courts should have to compel state agencies to act in situations like these is regrettable.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: High cost of incompetency
item.description: THE panic and chaos across the country last week over fuel pricing is yet another example of how growing incompetence and expanding government bureaucracy are harming Papua New Guinea. It also highlights how promises from political leaders carry little weight.
item.content:encoded:
THE panic and chaos across the country last week over fuel pricing is yet another example of how growing incompetence and expanding government bureaucracy are harming Papua New Guinea.

It also highlights how promises from political leaders carry little weight.

The prime minister committed to subsidising fuel prices for citizens.

Weeks later, despite efforts from stakeholders, subsidies were not paid, triggering nationwide panic.

If the money exists, why is the process not seamless?

Are we to trust that the assurances from leadership are accurate?

Black Wednesday, caused by a payroll glitch, resulted in hundreds of millions of Kina in business losses.

The Government promised support, yet affected businesses report receiving little or nothing.

Meanwhile, ministers posted photos from a hotel in Port Moresby, smiling to demonstrate they were “fixing” the fuel issue.

Such displays of complacency are shameful, especially when mismanagement continues under the treasurer’s watch.

Citizens must demand leaders who follow through on promises and manage resources responsibly.

As 2027 approaches, it is a chance to elect leaders capable of guiding PNG forward after 50 years of independence.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Clear signs of economic struggle
item.description: THE signs of a struggling country are clear. Public motor vehicle operators strike or halt services due to rising fuel prices, airlines cancel flights over aviation fuel shortages, citizens struggle to afford basic goods, students and parents face over K10,000 in university fees, and public servants’ salaries often delayed.
item.content:encoded:
THE signs of a struggling country are clear.

Public motor vehicle operators strike or halt services due to rising fuel prices, airlines cancel flights over aviation fuel shortages, citizens struggle to afford basic goods, students and parents face over K10,000 in university fees, and public servants’ salaries often delayed.

These issues are concrete evidence of a failing state.

To address these challenges, Papua New Guinea must strengthen governance, financial management, and accountability.

Effective management of public funds and resources is critical for national prosperity, just as it is for institutions or businesses.

Historically, gaps in institutional capacity and experience have undermined state management.

Learning from global best practices, improving oversight, and ensuring transparent use of resources can help restore trust and stability.

PNG needs bold, constructive reforms to build a system that delivers for its citizens.

It is time to act decisively to secure the country’s future and ensure resources are managed effectively for the benefit of all .

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Speeches not saving ordinary citizens
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea must stop becoming merely a venue for endless international conferences, summits, and political talkfests that deliver little real improvement for ordinary citizens. While Prime Minister James Marape continues welcoming foreign leaders and delegates, many Papua New Guineans ask: what meaningful benefits flow back to the people after these events?
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea must stop becoming merely a venue for endless international conferences, summits, and political talkfests that deliver little real improvement for ordinary citizens.

While Prime Minister James Marape continues welcoming foreign leaders and delegates, many Papua New Guineans ask: what meaningful benefits flow back to the people after these events?

For decades, PNG has hosted major regional and global gatherings, yet citizens still face daily hardships – unemployment, poor healthcare, deteriorating roads, unreliable electricity, rising food and fuel prices, worsening law and order, and limited opportunities for youth.

PNG must maintain strong international relationships, but high-level meetings must now produce practical, measurable outcomes.

Coastal communities should benefit from ocean conferences through better fisheries management, marine training programmes, local processing industries, and sustainable jobs.

Economic summits should provide local businesses, small-to-medium enterprises, farmers, and youth with direct access to investment, development programmes, and employment pathways.

Leadership must move beyond conference politics and focus on implementation, accountability, and service delivery.

True success is measured by improved living standards, infrastructure, healthcare, education, job creation, and economic empowerment – not by speeches or the number of foreign delegates.

Every international meeting hosted in PNG must produce tangible benefits for the people.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s frontier judges: peace-building from the bench
item.description: IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges. Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.
item.content:encoded:
IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges.

Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.

The scale of the challenge is hard to overstate. In Hela and Enga – two of the Highlands provinces hardest hit by inter-group conflict – sophisticated weaponry, warlord networks, and overwhelmed police have combined to produce a crisis that conventional state responses have struggled to contain.

Mobile squad deployments, special policing operations and draconian legislative announcements are all being experimented with.

But quietly, on the ground, judges are trying something different.

As reported in one of the daily newspapers on Feb 19, Justice Andrew Kostopolous, the resident National Court Judge in Hela, used a court-sanctioned inquiry into tribal fighting and lawlessness to confront the province’s deepening governance crisis head-on.

In proceedings marked by unusual candour, he issued bans on alcohol and the public carrying of bush knives, ordered the surrender of firearms, and named specific warlords as criminals subject to immediate arrest.

Justice Kostopolous also issued a human rights violation order against opposing factions in a tribal fight near Tari town, and delivered a stern warning that the province was “teetering on the edge of a constitutional crisis”.

“If the police aren’t doing their job, that’s a constitutional crisis,” he said.

“If the executive is not doing its job, that’s a constitutional crisis.”

These provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into “social engineers”.

The Supreme Court has affirmed that the Constitution is to be interpreted broadly, as “goal-oriented, purposive” and “liberal”, not in a “strict, technical and legalistic” manner.

Judges are urged to “think expansively and to be dynamic” and to “use judicial ingenuity to do justice”.

This is an unusual mandate, though not a unique one: judges in South Africa and India operate under similarly interventionist frameworks.

Three constitutional provisions in PNG are central to this logic.

Section 57 empowers the Supreme and National Courts to protect guaranteed rights and freedoms – either on application or on their own initiative.

Under this provision judges have summoned police to investigate alleged rights breaches, order the closure of prisons on humanitarian grounds, and directed law enforcement commissioners on the management of their facilities.

Critically, Section 57(5) extends this power to cases of “actual or imminent infringement”, allowing courts to make anticipatory orders before a rights breach has fully materialised.

Section 22 grants courts the power to enforce constitutional duties and to formulate procedures and mechanisms where existing frameworks are insufficient.

Section 225 obliges state agencies and service providers to actively support constitutional bodies, including the courts themselves.

Taken together, these provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into what legal scholar Bal Kama describes as “social engineers” – actors empowered to identify systemic failures and act to address them.

In Enga, resident judge Justice Graham Ellis has been equally active.

 Last month, he issued a series of orders on his own initiative aimed at stopping tribal fighting in the Laiagam area.

These included a ban on carrying machetes and axes in public, the establishment of police roadblocks in the Mulitaka area, and orders for police to arrest anyone breaching National Court directives.

He explicitly framed these interventions as a matter of human rights enforcement under Section 57 of the Constitution, noting that the fighting affected rights to life, freedom, protection of the law, freedom of assembly, and freedom of movement.

Justice Ellis’s reasoning is worth quoting at length, because it illuminates the distinctive logic that underpins these interventions: “In many parts of the world, judges can do no more than consider the effects of criminal conduct, notably conducting trials after it is alleged that a crime has been committed. However, the inclusion of human rights provisions in the Constitution of PNG enables a judge to take a more active role by making orders, where it is considered appropriate, that can try to stop and even prevent criminal conduct.”

Both judges are thinking systemically, not just case by case.

Rather than waiting for individual matters to come before them and then ruling on them in isolation, they are using the bench as a vantage point from which to map the conditions generating violence – and to issue orders aimed at disrupting them.

This is a direct challenge to what one judge has described to us as the conventional posture of the justice system: “We tend to come in after everything has happened.

“We wait until it has happened and then clean up the mess.”

item.title: Liberty Liko becomes fourth doctor in family
item.description: HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be. “Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”
item.content:encoded:
HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be.

“Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”

Her father is Dr Osbourne Liko, a surgeon and urologist, who also served as Health Secretary and Medical Board chairman.

Her mother Dr. Mary-Julian Baki is a pediatrician who had served in Gulf, Chimbu and Port Moresby for a total of 23 years. She is now in private practice.

Her brother Dr Malachi Liko graduated in 2020 with a Bachelor in Medicine, Bachelor in Surgery (MBBS) degree. He currently works as a general practitioner.

Her first cousin on her father’s side, Dr. Joyce Sonoling, is a psychiatrist.

On top of that, her family’s close friends include Dr Mathias Sapuri and his daughter Dr Yvonne Sapuri.

Liberty, 23, was among 400-plus students who graduated from the University of Papua New Guinea School of Medicine and Health Science last week.

She had her face painted in Kairuku to honor her Mum, who is the first from Waima in Kairuku to become a doctor.

Liberty wore a Morobe bilum and necklace to honor her Morobe heritage and father who is from Pindu in Finschaffen.

She dressed in the same way as her mother when she graduated from UPNG in 1992. It was to honour her and other women choosing the profession.

“Now that my dream is a reality, I am overwhelmed with gratitude, first and foremost, to our ever merciful God, and to my backbone, my parents.

“I am equally thankful to my entire support network as this achievement is just as much theirs as it is mine. I’ve never had to look far for good role models and I aspire to be even half the doctor my parents are.”

Her parents supported her education into university. She also secured a Government scholarship.

She attended primary school in Australia because of her mother’s overseas work attachment. She later attended the Port Moresby Grammar School.

She completed grades seven to 12 at the St Joseph International College in Port Moresby in 2019 before she was accepted into UPNG on an academic excellence scholarship in 2020. But Covid-19 affected her studies. So to get into medical school, she dropped the Science degree and withdrew from UPNG.

She enrolled at the Pacific Adventist University in 2021 for the Science Foundation with the aim of getting into the School of Medicine and Health Sciences. She attended the UPNG medical school from 2022 to 2025.

She will decide what branch of medicine she is to pursue once she completes her residency and internship.

Dad Dr Liko is proud of her achievement.

“Liberty earned her achievements on her own merits unequivocally. In fact, she had an enormous shoe to fill throughout her journey in pursuit of her medical career. Nearly every lecturer knew her, a majority of doctors and nurses knew who she was.

“But she has always been herself – level-headed, cool, calm and collective. She respects everybody despite any opinion they may have on her. She is always considerate of her less fortunate school mates whose parents are not in Port Moresby.

“She earned her MBBS through disciplined study, always have resilient study habits, perseverance and consistent hard work over five demanding years.

“Despite her family’s medical backgrounds and public roles, I’m proud to say that she never sought advantage or special treatment, choosing instead to uphold fairness and independence.”

Mum Dr Baki described Liberty as confident, determined and an intelligent young lady who always strives for perfection.

“She was no dainty girl growing up and always fought with her six older siblings to be heard. I am very proud of her as a mother and a female professional.”

But she knows well what lies ahead of Liberty.

“This is just the beginning of a long journey of sleepless nights, no weekends or public holidays, missing out on family events and sacrifice of personal time. It is a calling to serve.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
